title: DOM and asynchronous integration tests

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for DOM and asynchronous integration tests; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: DOM tests should query by accessible role, name, label, or text and exercise realistic user events and state changes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A test submits by button role and waits for an accessible status message instead of reading internal state. Your goal is to create deterministic tests for an accessible event and fetch workflow. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Browser state and security; Dependency boundaries; Pure and module tests; Cancellation, timeout and stale results; Forms, validation and accessible state. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: DOM tests should query by accessible role, name, label, or text and exercise realistic user events and state changes. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: jsdom differs from browsers, implementation selectors are brittle, and async updates require observable waiting. This draft therefore rejects: Tests should select generated class names because they identify exact implementation nodes.

includes: Test user-observable state, events, loading, failure and cancellation rather than implementation details.
Create deterministic tests for an accessible event and fetch workflow.
excludes: jsdom differs from browsers, implementation selectors are brittle, and async updates require observable waiting.

outcomes: Create deterministic tests for an accessible event and fetch workflow.

examples.title: DOM and asynchronous integration tests: concrete case
examples.situation: A test submits by button role and waits for an accessible status message instead of reading internal state.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A test submits by button role and waits for an accessible status message instead of reading internal state.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Query the interface as users perceive it and assert observable behavior, not generated classes or private state.
examples.result: The result follows because Query the interface as users perceive it and assert observable behavior, not generated classes or private state.

examples.title: DOM and asynchronous integration tests: boundary case
examples.situation: jsdom differs from browsers, implementation selectors are brittle, and async updates require observable waiting.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: jsdom differs from browsers, implementation selectors are brittle, and async updates require observable waiting.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Tests should select generated class names because they identify exact implementation nodes.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Query the interface as users perceive it and assert observable behavior, not generated classes or private state.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Tests should select generated class names because they identify exact implementation nodes.
misconceptions.correction: Query the interface as users perceive it and assert observable behavior, not generated classes or private state.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Tests should select generated class names because they identify exact implementation nodes.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model DOM and asynchronous integration tests, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for DOM and asynchronous integration tests before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create deterministic tests for an accessible event and fetch workflow. Keep this boundary in view: jsdom differs from browsers, implementation selectors are brittle, and async updates require observable waiting.
retrievalPrompts: Explain DOM and asynchronous integration tests using exact JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications terms.
Why is this claim incorrect: Tests should select generated class names because they identify exact implementation nodes.
Apply checkpoint 'users perceive it' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Modular browser application”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
